title: Print vs Println — spot the difference
instruction: `fmt.Print` stays on the same line, `fmt.Println` jumps to a new line. Run this and watch how they behave differently.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Print("Welcome ")
	fmt.Print("to the ")
	fmt.Print("library")
	fmt.Println()
	fmt.Println("You have 3 books checked out")
}
successMessage: Print stays put, Println moves down. Two tools, same toolbox.

title: Printf — format verbs
instruction: `fmt.Printf` uses format verbs like `%s` (string) and `%d` (int). Change the book title and page count to your own and run it.
hint: Change "The Great Gatsby" and 180 to your own values. Leave the format string as is.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	title := "The Great Gatsby"
	pages := 180
	fmt.Printf("Book: %s has %d pages\n", title, pages)
}

codeChecks.message: Keep the Printf format string as is. Only change the values.
Change the title from "The Great Gatsby" to your own.

successMessage: One line, multiple values, full control over how they appear.

title: One number, many faces 🤯
instruction: Go has a verb for everything: `%v` (value), `%T` (type), `%b` (binary), `%x` (hex). Run this — one number, five different ways.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	n := 42
	fmt.Printf("default:  %v\n", n)
	fmt.Printf("type:     %T\n", n)
	fmt.Printf("binary:   %b\n", n)
	fmt.Printf("hex:      %x\n", n)
	fmt.Printf("decimal:  %d\n", n)
}
successMessage: 42 in binary is 101010. In hex it's 2a. Same number, totally different faces. Computers are wild.

title: Width and alignment
instruction: `%10s` right-aligns in 10 spaces, `%-10s` left-aligns, `%05d` zero-pads. Run this to see columns line up.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Printf("%10s | %-10s\n", "Fiction", "Non-Fic")
	fmt.Printf("%10s | %-10s\n", "Sci-Fi", "History")
	fmt.Printf("%10s | %-10s\n", "Biog:", "042")
	fmt.Printf("Book #%05d\n", 42)
}
successMessage: Width and alignment make output readable. Think receipts, tables, logs.

title: Decimal precision
instruction: `%.2f` gives exactly 2 decimal places. A book costs $14.99, not $14.990000. Change `%f` to `%.2f` for price and `%.1f` for discount.
hint: Use %.2f for price and %.1f for discount.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	price := 14.99
	discount := 15.333333
	fmt.Printf("Price: $%f\n", price)
	fmt.Printf("Discount: %f%%\n", discount)
}

codeChecks.message: Use %.2f to format the price to 2 decimal places.
Use %.1f to format the discount to 1 decimal place.

successMessage: Clean numbers. Nobody wants to see $14.990000 on a price tag.

title: Sprintf — build a string, don't print it
instruction: `fmt.Sprintf` works like Printf but returns the result as a string. Build a message that says `"You returned \"1984\". It is due in 7 days."` and print it.
hint: message := fmt.Sprintf("You returned \"%s\". It is due in %d days.", title, 7) — then fmt.Println(message).

codeChecks.message: Use fmt.Sprintf to build the message string.
Store the result in a variable called message.
Replace the TODO lines with actual code.

successMessage: Sprintf is Printf that hands you the result instead of printing it.

title: Escape sequences
instruction: `\n` is a newline, `\t` is a tab, `\\` is a backslash, `\"` is a quote inside a string. Run this to see each one in action.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("Shelf A\nShelf B")
	fmt.Println("Genre:\tFiction")
	fmt.Println("Path: Library\\Archives\\Fiction")
	fmt.Println("She said \"overdue\"")
}
successMessage: Escape sequences let you put special characters inside strings. \n and \t are the ones you'll use most.
